llm_operation.reason: PyTorch's torch.normal requires 'std' parameter to be non-negative, and the original test case does not specify values — only shape/dtype — so std tensors may contain negative values, triggering 'normal expects all elements of std >= 0.0'. MindSpore's mindspore.ops.normal does not accept 'std' as a keyword argument; instead it uses 'stddev', and requires explicit 'shape' parameter (unlike PyTorch which infers output shape from mean/std broadcast). Also, PyTorch's torch.normal has two signatures: (mean, std) or (mean=..., std=...) but does not accept 'std' as standalone kwarg without 'mean'; however the original case omits actual tensor generation logic. To make both frameworks comparable and valid: (1) Generate concrete mean/std tensors with non-negative stddev values; (2) For PyTorch: use positional args (mean, std) or consistent kwargs (mean=..., std=...); (3) For MindSpore: replace 'std' → 'stddev', add mandatory 'shape' derived from broadcast semantics (here same as mean.shape), and ensure dtype compatibility. Also, MindSpore's normal requires shape to be a tuple, and mean/stddev must be broadcastable to that shape — so we set shape = mean.shape and ensure mean/stddev are scalars or compatible tensors. Since original case uses same shape for mean and std, safest repair is to use scalar mean/stddev + explicit shape in MindSpore, and match PyTorch's scalar-input usage (which broadcasts). But per docs, both support tensor inputs — so we fix by using valid non-negative std values and correct param names.
